context.description: Reflective prose argues that a person can gradually internalize and inhabit an opposing symbolic worldview, such as flat-earth belief, atheism, religion, or agnosticism. It describes the transition as a long, difficult process that can produce temporary instability between identities while a new worldview becomes convincing enough to serve as a personal foundation.
normalization_notes: Classified as a philosophy-of-consciousness book page focused on symbolic worldviews and identity change.
Translated visible German prose into English control-language summary.

rows.paragraph_text: A person can gradually adopt a different symbolic worldview by making its internal coherence feel natural and personally meaningful.
rows.text_content: The transition can briefly leave someone between identities, such as between atheism, religion, and agnosticism, before the new worldview becomes stable.

rows.paragraph_text: The method can be applied to opposing belief systems, including flat-earth belief and religious trust, by treating them as lived symbolic structures.
rows.text_content: Its success is measured by how consistent the new symbolism feels and whether one is willing to build a personality on it.

rows.paragraph_text: During the shift, a person may feel a strange split or instability because the old foundation has not been fully left behind.
rows.text_content: The passage warns that one may discover other symbolisms that suddenly feel closer than the target worldview.

rows.paragraph_text: Agnostic identity and reference to Henry Huxley appear as examples of drifting into a new orientation.
rows.text_content: The argument ends mid-sentence with a caution that direction can be lost along the way.
